attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old woman is brought into the Emergency Department with sudden onset blurring and loss of vision in her right eye. Her symptoms were preceded by a six-hour history of gradually worsening right-sided, throbbing headache, as well as a 1-month history of general malaise, myalgia, and intermittent fevers. She reports a previous episode of similar transient vision loss 1-month prior. There is no history of recent trauma. She has a past medical history of ischaemic heart disease and peripheral vascular disease. She is a current smoker with a 45 pack-year history. She is currently taking aspirin and atorvastatin as per her GP.

On examination, she is alert, orientated and haemodynamically stable. Examination shows a marked decrease in visual acuity in the right eye and she is unable to count fingers with her right eye. She is also found to have a marked relative afferent pupillary defect on the right side. A fundoscopic examination shows a chalky white swelling of the optic disc.
attributes.stemQuestion: Given the likely diagnosis, which one of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Temporal artery biopsy followed by IV methylprednisolone
attributes.explanation:
The diagnosis is giant cell arteritis (GCA), previously known as temporal arteritis, which is a vasculitis of large and medium-sized vessels. Features supportive of this diagnosis include headache, jaw claudication and tenderness to palpation over the artery. Constitutional symptoms may also be observed including fever, fatigue, and weight loss. Nevertheless, GCA may lead to arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy, which would manifest as a monocular visual loss, with a relative afferent pupillary defect and a chalky, white ischaemic disc on fundoscopy. Visual loss is rarely reversible once established. It is therefore imperative that if the diagnosis of GCA is suspected the patient is admitted immediately and given intravenous methylprednisolone.

Inflammatory markers such as erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are non-specific and have broad ranges. Normal levels do not exclude the diagnosis, although they make the diagnosis less likely. The diagnosis should be made on histopathology with a biopsy of the temporal artery or imaging studies - but there is no definitive diagnostic test for GCA. While checking ESR/CRP is part of the workup for suspected GCA, when there is an immediate threat to vision (as in this case with acute visual loss), corticosteroids should be initiated without delay, even before obtaining inflammatory marker results. In less urgent presentations without visual symptoms, it would be appropriate to check inflammatory markers before starting treatment.

Temporal artery biopsies are also subject to false-negative results (30-44%) due to the segmental nature of arteritis. Histologic findings are present for at least 1-month after steroid therapy has been instituted as the inflammatory infiltrate resolves slowly. Steroid treatment should not be withheld to await a biopsy.

Colour doppler ultrasound may be performed as a substitute for temporal artery biopsy as a diagnostic tool, although it is reliant on user experience. Imaging such as CT, CTA, PET, and MR angiography lack sufficient spatial resolution to visualise the temporal artery. In its high-resolution form (contrast-enhanced), its sensitivity and specificity is 78.4% and 90.4% respectively. However, its use is limited by the need for contrast and cost considerations.
learningPoints: Giant cell arteritis is a medical emergency, requiring prompt treatment with intravenous steroids.
When there is an immediate threat to vision in suspected giant cell arteritis, corticosteroids should be initiated without delay, even before confirmatory tests.
references: Ameer MA, Peterfy RJ, Bansal P, Khazaeni B. Temporal Arteritis.  StatPearls. Treasure Island (FL)2021.
Ashton-Key MR, Gallagher PJ. False-negative temporal artery biopsy. Am J Surg Pathol. 1992;16(6):634-5.

Duhaut P, Pinède L, Bornet H, Demolombe-Ragué S, Dumontet C, Ninet J, et al. Biopsy proven and biopsy negative temporal arteritis: differences in clinical spectrum at the onset of the disease. Groupe de Recherche sur l'Artérite à Cellules Géantes. Ann Rheum Dis. 1999;58(6):335-41.
Winkler A, True D. Giant Cell Arteritis: 2018 Review. Mo Med. 2018;115(5):468-70.
